A man sits at the narrow window counter of a modest street kiosk, looking out toward the sidewalk with a calm, attentive expression. The framing is tight — shot from the chest up at a slight angle — drawing focus to his thoughtful face and the intimate details of his surroundings. He wears a layered winter outfit: a worn dark leather jacket with soft fabric lining, a beige woolen sweater underneath, and a collared shirt visible at the neck. One arm rests on the countertop, while the other is relaxed. In front of him stands a clear glass of black tea, placed inside a traditional silver-colored metal glass holder — a 10 cm tall podstakannik, decorated with classic floral engraving, reminiscent of both Soviet train culture and everyday tea rituals of late 20th century Russia and Eastern Europe.

The counter also holds an open notebook with a pen casually dropped onto its pages. Behind him, the kiosk shelves are densely filled with nostalgic Soviet-era goods: a bright blue can of condensed milk (“sweetened condensed milk” with a geometric Soviet label), packs of “Marlboro” cigarettes stacked beside old Russian brands like “Belomorkanal” and “Kosmos,” chocolate bars with yellow and red wrappers, chewing gum “Turbo,” and boxes of matches. The atmosphere is steeped in the 90s aesthetic — a time capsule of the early post-Soviet streets. Taped to the window is a handwritten bilingual notice reading “NO CHANGE”, along with faded notes about cigarettes and prices.

The light is soft and indirect, filtered through the kiosk glass, casting a natural glow on the man’s face and highlighting textures — the shine of the podstakannik, the matte paper, the scuffed wood. Outside the kiosk, a blurred urban street is faintly visible. The mood is quiet, observational, and real — evoking the gritty charm of 90s Eastern Europe. Shot with a 50mm lens, documentary framing, natural light. A cinematic moment full of stillness, realism, and subtle irony.

Flux promt: A man is photographed from the waist up, standing in front of an old Soviet car, leaning with one arm on the open driver’s side door. He wears a worn-out, oil-stained white tank top and dark sweatpants. His expression is weary but alert, as if caught mid-day in the middle of repairs. His clothing clings to his body, streaked with motor oil and grime. The scene is set in a rugged, cluttered auto yard with stacks of used tires, rusted corrugated metal garage doors and a scattering of tools and debris behind him.

The lighting is soft and diffused from a cloudy sky, eliminating harsh shadows and adding a flat, overcast quality to the image. This creates a subdued tone, emphasizing the textures of his stained shirt, dirty skin, and the scratched surfaces of the old vehicle. The colors are muted: grays, worn greens, and dull browns dominate the environment.

Shot at eye level with a standard focal length, the framing is tight, keeping attention on the man’s upper body and facial expression while using background elements to situate him in a gritty post-Soviet context. The photograph evokes 90s Eastern Europe — raw, unpolished, and grounded in a working-class reality. The visual narrative is documentary-like, stripped of any artificial stylization, focused on realism and texture, with an atmosphere of physical labor and resilience.

A man stands in the middle of a Soviet-era concrete courtyard, surrounded by worn-down apartment blocks with faded facades and broken balconies. He wears a fitted black T-shirt and a thick short gold chain necklace that rests closely around the base of his neck. On his wrist is a large white-faced watch. His pose is assertive — both hands lifting the chain slightly, elbows bent, shoulders square. The camera is close and frontal, capturing his upper body and confident, steady expression. The lighting is warm and low, casting rich shadows and creating cinematic contrast. The background is slightly blurred, keeping focus on his figure. The atmosphere is stylized and moody, evoking the aesthetics of early 2000s action cinema colliding with post-Soviet street realism. Shot on a 50mm lens, natural hard light, sunset tones, gritty texture. Raw street attitude, urban post-Soviet aesthetic. Cinematic photography by Steven Klein, street portrait meets retro gangster visual.

A man sits alone at a wooden kitchen table in a modest Soviet-style apartment, bathed in golden afternoon sunlight streaming through a half-closed window. He wears a sleeveless striped blue and white tank top — the iconic Russian telnyashka — and intently rewinds a tape with a pencil, a nostalgic gesture from a bygone era. His pose is grounded and calm, as if the outside world has stopped. His gaze is directed straight into the camera, quietly inviting the viewer to bear witness to the quiet intimacy of the moment.

On the table next to him are familiar markers of a simple yet symbolic ritual: a bottle of clear vodka with a Soviet-style red label reading “VODKA,” a full glass jar of pickles with the brine visible, and a small faceted shot glass, partially filled. The tabletop glows warmly in the sunlight, highlighting the texture of the glass, the tape, and the man’s forearm.

Behind him is a dimly lit kitchen with old floral wallpaper faded with time. Just above him hangs a framed portrait of Stalin, adding irony and surrealism to the ordinary domestic setting. An old gas stove and an aluminum kettle on it speak of the simplicity and austerity of the interior. A lace curtain covers part of a small window, letting in just enough light to contrast with the shadows and brightness throughout the room.

The entire scene conveys the atmosphere of a quiet day in Russia in the 1980s or 90s — filled with silence, memories, and a touch of the absurd. Shot in a narrow-angle composition with medium focus, this portrait conveys a deeply defined mood: the intersection of male loneliness, post-Soviet melancholy, and cult ritual. The lighting, composition and symbolism reflect the raw aesthetic of Gregory Halpern or documentary footage from post-industrial Eastern Europe.

A man sits on a red-painted iron swing surrounded by a playground with a metal slide and sandbox in an old Soviet courtyard, against a backdrop of five-story panel apartment buildings. The shot is composed as a knee-length portrait, taken at a 45-degree angle using a 50mm lens with a shallow depth of field - the building and background fade into blur, creating an airy bokeh effect. It is late evening, and the warm glow of the setting sun casts long shadows and golden hues on the concrete ground.

The man wears casual, worn clothes typical of the late Soviet era: loose dark sweatpants made of slightly wrinkled fabric, a soft burgundy sweater buttoned halfway up, and an unbuttoned brown parka with a modest fabric hood framing the back of his head. In a relaxed pose, he leans slightly forward on the swing, knees apart. One hand rests between his legs, holding the neck of an unopened bottle of vodka – the Cyrillic label “STOLICHNAYA” printed crisply on white paper with red accents, evoking unmistakable Soviet iconography.

The reflection of his face is introspective, his gaze looking away from the camera, lost in thought. There is no sign of performance or posing – just a quiet stillness, a moment caught between nostalgia and reality. The iron chains of a swing frame his back, their paint peeling with age. The ground beneath is cracked asphalt, dotted with tufts of dry, softly glowing grass from the remaining sun.

The scene conveys the raw mood of post-Soviet stillness – a melancholy yet warm atmosphere where light, texture and silence become carriers of meaning. The style echoes the documentary realism of Gregory Halpern, where Soviet iconography and intimate portraiture merge into a quiet, symbolic image.

A candid Soviet-era scene captured at a 45-degree side angle with a shallow depth of field, shot on a 85mm f/1.4 lens, focusing on a man preparing a glass of tomato juice at a self-service station in a grocery store. The background is softly blurred, revealing indistinct rows of canned goods and preserves stacked in faded blue-green metal shelves. The man is dressed in modest, realistic late-Soviet winter attire: a grey wool overcoat with large buttons, worn over a dark turtleneck or sweater, and a traditional black ushanka hat with lowered earflaps. His hand stirs salt into the glass with a spoon, while three more glasses of tomato juice stand nearby on the metal counter, next to a sugar dispenser and a small dish with white salt.

The lighting is soft and diffused, coming from overhead fluorescent fixtures, casting even shadows with a cool indoor tone. His pose is slightly hunched forward, focused on the motion of the spoon, creating an atmosphere of quiet concentration and daily routine. The framing captures him from head to just above the knees, with his upper body in sharp focus while the shelves behind fade into blur. His gaze is turned downward, adding to the understated realism of the moment.

The color palette is muted and authentically Soviet — greys, olive greens, dusty reds and glassy browns, evoking a nostalgic and utilitarian aesthetic. The photograph evokes a sense of stillness and dry humor, a slice-of-life moment from the late USSR where tomato juice with salt was an everyday ritual.

A candid Soviet street scene from the 1950s or early 1960s, captured on color film with visible grain, soft natural light, and slightly faded vintage tones typical of amateur photography of the time. The image is taken at a slight upward angle, showing a man standing in front of a dark, polished Soviet car, with reflections of the street visible in the chrome details.

The man is casually dressed in a mid-century Soviet outfit: he wears a light grey jacket over an unbuttoned blue-checkered shirt and darker high-waisted trousers with a relaxed fit. On his head is a soft brown beret, tilted slightly to the side. Slung over his left shoulder is a tan leather camera bag with a long strap. He holds a vintage Soviet film camera with a large lens in his hands, adjusting the settings with a concentrated but cheerful expression, his gaze directed to the right, as if chatting with someone out of frame.

His stance is relaxed, with one leg slightly bent and his weight shifted onto the other. The lighting is natural and diffused, likely an overcast summer day, with soft shadows under the car. The overall atmosphere feels friendly, warm, and documentary, offering a nostalgic glimpse into the everyday fashion, technology, and casual social moments of the Soviet Union. The color palette includes greys, browns, and pale blues, reinforcing the vintage realism of the scene.

A three-quarter portrait of a man standing in a worn Soviet courtyard, captured with a 50mm lens at waist level, using soft daylight with mild overcast conditions. The background shows a weathered Khrushchyovka-style apartment block with visible cracks in the plaster, faded paint, and two small wooden-framed windows — typical Soviet-era architecture. The lighting is natural, casting soft shadows and highlighting the washed-out textures of the scene.

The man wears a vintage 1990s color-block windbreaker in the style of FILA, with a zipped front and a combination of dark teal, navy, and burgundy panels. Underneath, he has on a ribbed camel-colored sweater. His jeans are classic mid-wash denim with a high rise, slightly loose fit, and subtle fading on the thighs.

His posture is frontal and relaxed, both arms hanging naturally by his sides. In his right hand, he holds a cream-colored string bag (avoska) by the handle — only the loop is visible, but the netting suggests it’s filled with everyday groceries. Inside the bag, partially visible near the top, are a bottle of Moskovskaya vodka, a bright tea box, and vintage matchboxes with Soviet-style illustrations.

The overall palette is composed of washed-out earth tones, saturated retro color blocks, and the neutral greys of concrete and plaster. The scene recreates the authentic aesthetic of early post-Soviet life — casual, grounded, and textured — evoking the candid look of analog film photography in 1992.

A cinematic, overcast street scene in post-Soviet Russia, shot with a 50mm lens on analog film for a vintage, soft-focus look with subtle grain and low contrast. A young man stands beside an old black Volga sedan with the driver’s side door open, just stepping out of the car. His posture is casual but alert, with one hand resting on the open door and the other near his waist. He looks directly into the camera with a neutral, slightly confident expression, a cigarette hanging from the corner of his lips. His gaze adds a sense of tension and narrative, like a paused moment from a noir crime film.

He wears a patterned Soviet-style wool sweater in cream and brown, tucked into high-waisted plaid trousers with a wide belt, and a dark blue denim jacket with prominent chest pockets. His outfit reflects the fashion of working-class youth in the 1980s USSR, with practical materials and modest tailoring. The jacket is slightly worn, with a stiff collar and visible fading on the seams.

The background features Soviet-era industrial buildings and gray corrugated metal sheds, slightly out of focus, with two tall red-and-white smokestacks releasing clouds of smoke into the overcast sky. The ground is wet and reflective from recent rain, enhancing the moody, cold atmosphere.

The entire frame feels like a still from a 1980s gangster drama or detective story, with muted colors, desaturated tones, and a strong sense of place. The scene should evoke mystery, unease, and a moment suspended in time.

A candid street photograph taken in the late Soviet Union on a sunny day, shot on 35mm color film with visible grain, slightly washed-out tones, and overexposed highlights typical of 1980s amateur photography. The image is taken at medium height from a short distance, with natural daylight casting harsh shadows and enhancing the vivid color contrasts.

A young man walks along the sidewalk next to a red intercity bus and a parked Soviet car. He is wearing a bright turquoise short-sleeved polo shirt with dark blue and white geometric stripes and dark trousers. The shirt is tucked in and a plastic watch is visible on his left wrist. In his right hand he carries a bouquet of white, red, wildflowers wrapped in a sheet of newspaper.

His pose is quick and expressive, he walks forward, frowning or squinting slightly in the sunlight, looking ahead. In the background is the side of a large red bus and a white and grey Soviet car with yellow on black license plates. The lower foreground is partially washed out with green, adding depth and a sense of randomness.

The color palette features bright Soviet-era hues - bright blue, faded red, asphalt grey - combined with the soft texture of old film. The scene conveys the spontaneity and documentary nature of everyday life in the USSR, with a hint of humor and sincerity in the gesture of offering flowers carelessly wrapped in newsprint.

COMMUNAL KITCHEN, MAN WITH EGG AND NEWSPAPER
Three-quarter portrait of a man sitting in a worn communal kitchen, captured at knee level. He wears a faded plaid bathrobe over a stretched-out white tank top, with a slightly crooked knit beanie on his head. He holds a half-peeled boiled egg in one hand and a spoon in the other, paused mid-bite. A Soviet newspaper lies folded in front of him on a floral oilcloth-covered table. Behind him, cracked white tile with missing grout, an enamel teapot on the stovetop, a chipped saucepan, and a narrow wooden shelf holding a glass jar of instant coffee labeled “India.” A thin curtain lets in soft daylight from a high window. His expression is calm and resigned, with a slight amused smirk, as he looks into the camera. The atmosphere is quiet, textured, and distinctly late-Soviet. Natural light, vertical frame, shot on 50mm with shallow depth of field.

Three-quarter portrait of a man in a late-Soviet kitchen, standing at a table and slicing a thick stick of boiled sausage placed horizontally across a worn wooden cutting board. He wears a dark knit pullover with wide cream-colored V-shaped stripes running diagonally from shoulders to waist, and underneath — a small-checkered shirt with the collar peeking out. On him are also loose dark blue cotton sweatpants with stretched knees, evoking the relaxed homewear of the era. He looks into the camera with a warm, inviting smile, as if preparing for guests or helping his wife. On the table, next to the cutting board, lies a white enamel plate with a delicate blue pattern, already holding several neat slices of sausage. Around the plate — a small pile of chopped spring onions, a few white porcelain soup bowls, and stacked ceramic dessert plates. In the background, the light-green square-tiled wall shows age and wear, with a gas stove against it, a large enamel saucepan on top, and mismatched drawers and counters in pale wood. A rope stretches diagonally across the kitchen above the stove, with various pieces of laundry hanging — undershirts, baby bodysuits, a short-sleeved plaid shirt — drying in the rising warmth from the burners. The lighting is natural, coming from the window on the left, casting soft shadows and a nostalgic glow over the room. The scene captures the atmosphere of everyday domestic life in the USSR with humor, affection and realism. Cropped at the knees, vertical frame.

A three-quarter length portrait of a man walking confidently down the street of a Soviet city on a summer day. He is wearing a short-sleeved red and yellow checked button-down shirt with a narrow and elongated collar in the 70s style, tucked into high-waisted grey wool trousers with pleats in the front, slightly wrinkled and careless. A simple, slightly worn brown leather belt and a classic wristwatch on a brown leather strap complete his look. In his hand he carries a mesh bag filled with groceries - glass jars, a loaf of dark bread and several round onions are visible inside. Behind him, in a blurred background, parked vintage Soviet cars in blue and beige tones Gaz, Moskvich. The light is bright and clear, casting natural shadows on his face and clothes. The camera is slightly tilted, capturing the man from knee level upwards, with a shallow cinematic depth of field. The atmosphere seems calm and focused, evoking the rhythm of everyday life in the USSR, with a subtle dignity and visual clarity. The shot is vertical, with realistic film grain.

A man in a 1990s Eastern European courtyard leans against the hood of a car, holding a wooden baseball bat over his shoulder. He wears a dark green and burgundy Soviet-style nylon tracksuit jacket zipped up to the collar. The lighting is warm and moody, with an overcast sky diffused across the concrete buildings in the background, rendered in soft bokeh. His expression is serious, bordering on defiant, with a fixed gaze directly into the camera. The framing is a portrait to the waist, emphasizing the stance and attitude of a small-town tough guy. The scene is gritty but cinematic, evoking the aesthetics of post-Soviet VHS tapes — retro color grading with a touch of film grain.

medium shot, knee-up composition; a young woman stands firmly at the center of a Soviet-era public gathering, looking directly into the camera with calm determination and unwavering composure. Her posture is straight and poised, both arms relaxed by her sides, projecting dignity and silent strength. The image captures a collective moment of ideological unity and pride, set in an open square beneath a clear blue sky. Behind her, a crowd of men and women of varied ethnic backgrounds stands together, holding vivid red flags emblazoned with the Soviet hammer-and-sickle symbol, waving dynamically in the wind.

She wears a tailored, full-length military-brown wool coat with structured shoulders and classic lapels, evoking the clean, functional elegance of mid-20th century Soviet fashion. Draped diagonally across her chest is a bold red satin sash, adorned with a single golden USSR emblem — the iconic hammer and sickle — centered without any additional lettering. The gleaming symbol subtly catches the light, adding ceremonial weight without distraction.

The scene is captured using a medium telephoto lens, allowing the subject to appear prominent and focused against a softly blurred yet detailed backdrop. The lighting is natural, with sunlight falling from above and slightly to the front, casting soft, directional shadows that define the textures of fabric and skin. Her face is evenly lit, revealing lifelike skin tone and texture with no excessive stylization—just clarity, youth, and raw realism.

The framing is tight, symmetrical, and purposeful. The minimalistic composition, combined with the sea of fluttering red behind her and the subtle variety in faces, creates a cinematic image steeped in ideological symbolism and visual storytelling. The atmosphere is formal and emotionally charged, recalling archival imagery of patriotic unity and generational conviction. Color palette: rich Soviet red, muted olive brown, pale skin tones, golden highlights, and sky blue. A modern tribute to the aesthetics of Soviet visual power, rendered in contemporary fashion-realism.

medium shot, knee-up composition; the model stands closer to the camera, centrally positioned and fully facing forward, her body aligned squarely with the lens. She gazes directly into the camera with a calm, composed expression — a portrait of quiet strength and presence. Her pose is upright, shoulders relaxed, arms naturally by her sides, capturing an honest and grounded stillness.

The setting is an authentic Soviet-era kitchen, captured with high realism. The background reveals a small, well-used kitchen space with faded geometric wallpaper, a laminate table covered with a floral oilcloth, a gas stove with an enamel kettle, and a wooden cupboard displaying mismatched glassware and tea tins. A lace curtain hangs softly over the window, through which the distant silhouette of the Kremlin is visible, grounding the domestic scene in historical context.

She is dressed in authentic Soviet everyday clothing — a woolen dress or skirt-and-blouse set in muted, practical tones like dusty burgundy, olive green, or deep brown. The tailoring is modest, with long sleeves and simple buttons, and she may wear a plain apron tied at the waist. The fabric appears textured and heavy, reflecting the functionality of the time.

The lighting is soft, natural daylight coming from the side window, casting a diffused glow over her face and garment while gently illuminating the warm tones of the kitchen around her. No harsh shadows — only natural contrast that brings out the subtle textures of fabric, wallpaper, and woodgrain.

The atmosphere is intimate, timeless, and emotionally resonant — a quiet moment of domestic life framed with dignity and realism. The color palette includes warm beige, pale sky blue, aged wood brown, muted Soviet green, and gentle red accents from enamelware. This cinematic portrait captures not just a figure, but a lived environment — filled with memory, culture, and historical authenticity.

A woman stands at the side of a long wooden dining table in a sunlit classical Moscow apartment with tall windows and carved moldings. She adjusts a silver spoon beside a porcelain plate, her smile open and peaceful, her gaze directed slightly past the lens. She wears a white midi-length dress with voluminous sleeves and a cinched waist, delicate broderie anglaise along the collar and cuffs.
On the table: a linen tablecloth with traditional embroidery, crystal goblets, vintage silverware, pastel-dyed eggs scattered between taper candles, and a tall kulich with dripping glaze and colorful sprinkles. A fresh bouquet of tulips and apple blossoms adds movement.
The photo is shot at 50mm from a slight angle, cropped at the knees, with the light naturally wrapping her face and hands. The scene is tasteful, young, and quietly reverent — a modern woman creating beauty through tradition.

This is a three-quarter portrait of a woman seated at a richly decorated Easter table, in a sunlit country kitchen with soft, pastel tones and blooming branches above. She looks directly at the camera with a bright, heartfelt smile, holding a small fluffy yellow chick gently in both hands, her fingers curved delicately to support the soft shape. Her expression is full of joy, warmth, and playful tenderness, inviting the viewer into this moment of celebration.

She wears a sheer blush-pink dotted organza blouse with a high ruffled neckline and short layered sleeves, paired with a pale linen skirt not visible in frame. Her long hair is left flowing naturally, brushed over her shoulders, adorned with a few tiny fresh flowers pinned at the temple. Her makeup is soft and luminous, capturing the essence of spring freshness.

The table in front of her is set with festive abundance: large pastel-blue cake stands stacked with hand-painted Easter eggs in pale yellow, lilac, sky blue and sage; a pair of miniature ceramic bunnies sit between them. A dome-shaped kulich dusted with powdered sugar rests on an etched glass plate in the center, surrounded by small marzipan birds, edible blossoms, and gold-wrapped eggs. To the left, a vase with blooming cherry branches rises above the table, adorned with hanging translucent Easter ornaments in pale pink, amber, and mint. The right side of the table features a woven nest centerpiece with dyed quail eggs, and behind it, two crystal glasses with peach rosé and light catching silver rims.

The scene is bathed in soft, diffused daylight coming from a side window, creating a gentle glow on the woman’s face and the reflective surfaces of the glass and ceramic. Shot on a 50mm f/1.4 lens with shallow depth of field, the focus is crisp on the woman’s smile and the chick in her hands, while the background remains dreamily blurred.

Styled for a Grazia Russia Easter feature, this editorial captures the joyful, magical charm of spring rebirth through pastel textures, tactile details, and the warmth of living tradition.

This is a three-quarter portrait capturing a woman seated at a round breakfast table by the panoramic window of the Ritz-Carlton Moscow. She smiles softly and warmly, looking directly into the camera with a light, joyful expression that reflects the peaceful luxury of Easter morning.

The woman is wearing a pale beige cashmere knit dress by Loro Piana with a semi-fitted silhouette and soft V-neckline, paired with a silk robe in champagne color, loosely tied at the waist. The robe’s cuffs and lapels are edged with tonal piping, and the fluid fabric drapes gently over her shoulders. Her hair is loosely gathered at the nape, and she wears thin gold hoop earrings from Maria Tash. The look is elegant, tactile, and understated — a relaxed luxury suited for a private suite breakfast.

In front of her, on the white-clothed table, stands a tall Easter kulich — 25 cm high and 15 cm in diameter — covered with thick white sugar glaze that drips down its golden crust. It’s topped with sugared violets and crushed pistachios. Surrounding the kulich are six vibrantly dyed eggs in matte lacquered shades of ruby, lilac, jade, and lemon, placed on a round porcelain tray by Hermès with golden edging. A slice of kulich rests nearby on a small dessert plate, showing raisins inside and a swirl of icing.

Next to the tray is a glass teapot filled with golden tea and a matching double-walled cup, as well as a small crystal vase with a sprig of blooming cherry blossom. In the background, the blurred silhouettes of the Kremlin towers and Hotel National can be seen through the panoramic window, bathed in morning light.

The scene is shot with a 50mm lens in soft natural daylight from the right, with the focus on the woman’s face and the Easter table elements, creating a balanced composition of intimacy and celebration. This image captures a moment of refined Moscow elegance and contemporary Orthodox tradition — a luxurious interpretation of Pascha.

This is a close-up portrait capturing a woman in a softly lit dressing room on the morning of Orthodox Easter.
The woman is wearing a cream satin blouse with a softly structured silhouette, long sleeves gathered at the wrist, a rounded collar, and delicate pearl buttons. The blouse is tucked into high-waisted tailored trousers in pale rose pink with pressed front pleats and a satin waistband. Her hair is styled in loose, polished waves pulled back with a golden barrette, and she wears small gold hoop earrings and a thin chain necklace.

She looks directly into the camera with a radiant and gentle smile, her expression filled with calm excitement and tenderness. In her hands, she carefully holds a white pastry box made of textured cardboard, tied with a pale silk ribbon. The lid is slightly open, revealing a festive Easter arrangement: a small traditional kulich covered in thick white sugar glaze that slowly drips over the sides, topped with pastel sugar pearls and a few sprigs of edible dried flowers. Around the cake are a handful of hand-dyed Easter eggs in a soft, matte palette — dusty rose, sage green, lilac grey — nestled into a bed of natural straw.

Behind her, the softly blurred background reveals a backlit mirror surrounded by glowing round bulbs, a crystal perfume bottle mid-spray, and a small vase with mimosa branches adding a festive spring touch.
The portrait is taken with a 50mm lens, using soft natural morning light filtered through curtains for flattering, diffused illumination.
Photographed by Ilya Varlamov for Glamour Russia, this frame captures an intimate, elegant mood of anticipation — a visual blend of modern femininity, festive ritual, and personal style.

This is a three-quarter portrait capturing a woman arranging an Easter brunch table in a minimalist Moscow loft.
The woman is wearing a crisp ivory poplin shirt with voluminous sleeves and a structured collar, paired with high-waisted beige culottes. Her hair is neatly tied into a sleek bun. Her joyful, relaxed smile and direct gaze into the camera create an inviting and celebratory mood.

She stands at the head of a concrete island countertop, gently adjusting a sprig of flowers atop a cylindrical Easter kulich — 15 cm tall — with her left hand, while her right hand rests on a wooden serving utensil placed near the base. The kulich is covered in thick white glaze that drips unevenly and is decorated with shards of candied citrus peel and miniature daisies. A footed ceramic bowl nearby holds pastel eggs in robin’s egg blue, butter yellow, and coral pink.

Behind her, a vase with blossoming pussy willow branches adds a seasonal accent, and a soft cotton napkin with Slavic embroidery lies beneath the pedestal. The space is lit by a high skylight with clean, directional morning light that casts sharp shadows and enhances the texture of the linen and ceramic surfaces. On the wall in the distance, a vintage-style poster reads “Happy Easter!” in red.

Photographed with a 50mm lens at f/2.8 by Katya Turkina for The Village Easter interiors series, this frame combines contemporary loft minimalism with reverent seasonal touches.

This is a close portrait capturing a woman standing in a stylized fashion studio set decorated with oversized branches of blossoming pussy willow.
The woman is wearing an architectural white cotton blouse by Bevza with exaggerated geometric sleeves and a sharply structured peplum. Her skirt is an asymmetrical piece by Maison Margiela, and she wears matte red lipstick.

She holds a vintage silver dish with a single kulich, its white sugar glaze dripping asymmetrically, topped with edible pearls and one miniature willow branch as a topper. Surrounding the cake are four eggs in bold Gzhel-inspired blue and white floral patterns.

The background is clean light gray, with shadows from the willow branches falling diagonally across her figure. The light is cold studio flash from the upper right, emphasizing the sculptural elements. Captured on a 50mm lens, f/2.0, high clarity.

Created for Numero Russia, this fashion editorial plays on Orthodox symbology through stylized minimalism and couture.

This is a medium-up portrait of a woman standing confidently behind a natural wood table in a softly lit daylight photography studio. She faces the camera with a radiant, welcoming smile, her eyes bright and expressive, as her right hand gently places the final dyed egg beside a traditional kulich. Her left hand rests delicately on the edge of the table, creating a poised and natural gesture of participation.

She is wearing a fitted white dress in clean minimalist tailoring, featuring soft cap sleeves and a subtle pleat through the bodice, adding movement to the otherwise structured silhouette. Her hair is styled in a sleek ponytail that keeps the focus on her joyful expression, and she wears classic drop earrings that add a festive touch to her refined appearance.

The tabletop is carefully styled in symmetrical balance: two tall Easter kulichi in the center, each cylindrical and perfectly risen, topped with thick white glaze that drips slightly down the sides and dusted generously with multicolored sugar confetti. To the right, a large ceramic plate holds hand-painted eggs in warm jewel tones — ruby, coral, amber, teal, lilac and ivory — arranged in a loose, abundant cluster. To the left, a low wicker basket contains more eggs in matching hues. Pussy willow stems in a small clear vase add vertical rhythm, and two empty crystal wine glasses are placed near the edges, framing the scene with elegant transparency.

Lighting is set to emulate soft natural window light, with a large diffused softbox positioned to the left, casting gentle shadows and highlighting the glaze of the kulichi and the eggshells’ matte texture. The woman’s face is crisply lit, with catchlights in her eyes and even skin tone.

Shot on a 50mm lens at f/2.0 for a shallow depth of field: the woman’s face and hands are in sharp focus, while the background — a studio curtain in light beige and soft reflectors — blurs softly, creating a warm, serene atmosphere.

This editorial image was styled and composed for ELLE Russia’s Easter feature, celebrating the harmony of fashion and tradition through modern portraiture and elegant visual storytelling.

It’s a three-quarter-length portrait of a woman standing in a sunny studio kitchen bathed in warm afternoon light. She’s looking slightly straight at the camera with a beaming, festive smile, holding a giant glass salad bowl filled to the brim with dozens of brightly colored Easter eggs—a playful, almost surreal twist on the traditional egg basket. The woman is wearing a luxury silk pajama set by Burberry in pastel pink with crisp white vertical stripes, consisting of a relaxed short-sleeved button-down top and matching shorts. Her hair is pulled back into a sleek low ponytail, and her earrings are large candy-shaped studs made of enamel and gold.

The kitchen is decorated in the style of a modern editorial shoot—sleek white cabinets, a partially visible marble island behind it, and a pink vintage Smeg refrigerator in the corner, adorned with Orthodox Easter stickers. On the counter next to her is a small white cake stand holding a miniature Easter cake, covered in glossy white icing and topped with a sugar bow and edible pearls. Nearby is a jar of glitter, scattered across the marble surface as if in the process of being decorated.

The photo is lit by bright, directional sunlight from a side window, casting dramatic shadows and highlights that highlight the texture and shine. Shot on a 50mm f/1.4 lens, the focus is on her smile and the bowl filled with eggs, with the background slightly out of focus.

Shot for the April cover of Grazia Russia, the concept combines Orthodox Easter nostalgia with modern glamour and humor – a viral-ready editorial that makes the humble egg the symbol of the season.

Neo-Folk Orthodox Portrait (Female, Egg Painting – Contemporary Hair)
A waist-up portrait in neofolk style captures a woman seated indoors on Orthodox Easter, facing the camera directly with a soft, serene smile. The setting is rustic and warm, with a wooden log wall as the background, softly lit by golden afternoon light from the side. She wears a traditional natural linen dress with a high neckline and long sleeves, adorned with red embroidery across the chest and cuffs in traditional Slavic patterns. Her hairstyle is modern and loose — a contemporary touch that subtly contrasts with the heritage garments, creating a refined dialogue between past and present.

In her right hand she holds a thin brush for egg painting, while her left hand gently supports a red Easter egg, keeping the hands clearly separated. The egg is decorated with fine golden floral patterns. On the table in front of her stands a ceramic bowl filled with red dye and several half-decorated eggs, suggesting an ongoing, thoughtful ritual.

The composition is centered and calm, shot vertically from the waist up with a 50mm lens. The shallow depth of field keeps the focus on her face and the egg she holds, while the background remains slightly blurred, enhancing the intimate atmosphere. The overall lighting is soft and directional, revealing the texture of the linen fabric and the gentle highlights on her face. The mood is quiet, contemplative, and rooted in tradition—yet clearly seen through a contemporary lens.

Three-quarter portrait of a woman seated on a wide windowsill in a softly lit Moscow apartment, wrapped in a light floral pajama set with loose-fitting sleeves and delicate piping. She holds a porcelain teacup in both hands, smiling gently as she looks slightly toward the camera, her gaze full of quiet joy and reflection. Her bare feet are tucked beneath her, and a soft knit blanket covers part of her legs.

The window beside her is framed by semi-sheer curtains, through which soft spring daylight filters in. On the sill next to her: a small white ceramic plate holds a single slice of traditional panettone-style kulich — golden-crumbed, dotted with raisins and bright candied fruits, the top thickly covered with white sugar glaze that drips slightly over the cut edge. The texture is rich and home-baked.

Beside the plate stands a slender beeswax candle in a vintage brass holder, its flame calm and steady. Two dyed eggs — one pale lilac, the other dusty rose — rest on a folded linen napkin embroidered with birds and branches. A small bouquet of fresh narcissus in a narrow glass vase adds a bright yellow note to the scene.

A sketchbook lies open on her other side, showing a watercolor drawing of Easter eggs. A pen and a stack of handwritten postcards complete the composition. The lighting is soft and natural, with a gentle glow across her face and hands. Shot on a 50mm lens, cropped just below the knees, the focus is tight on her expression and the slice of kulich.

The atmosphere is personal, poetic, and full of stillness — a gentle Easter morning lived in thought and detail.

Three-quarter portrait of a woman standing solemnly behind a ceremonial Easter table, her hands gently cradling a large blue-and-white porcelain bowl containing a tall panettone-style kulich glazed with thick white icing and topped with delicate sugar flowers. Her gaze is directed toward the camera with calm intensity, a faint, reverent smile softening her features.

She wears a structured midi-length cotton dress with a high collar and long sleeves, fully printed in traditional Gzhel-style blue floral and arabesque motifs on a milky white background. The silhouette is sculptural and slightly flared, cinched at the waist, reminiscent of Ulyana Sergeenko’s theatrical Slavic couture. Her hair is parted in the center and pulled into a tight braided crown, and her only accessory is a pair of cobalt blue ceramic earrings shaped like teardrops.

The table in front of her is covered with a white tablecloth embroidered with cobalt thread in floral cross-stitch patterns. It holds twelve porcelain saucers — each with one hand-painted Easter egg in shades of blue and indigo — symmetrically placed around the central kulich. Two tall beeswax candles in Gzhel candleholders flank the centerpiece. The entire porcelain set — teapot, goblets, vases — carries matching cobalt floral patterns. To the left, a tall white vase holds three thin pussy willow branches.

The background is a plaster wall painted in white with cobalt borders, echoing Gzhel panel motifs. The lighting is strong and directional from the left, creating deep blue shadows and a high-contrast editorial effect.

Shot with a 50mm lens, cropped just below the knees, the focus remains on her face and the porcelain bowl, while the ornate setting falls into painterly softness. The atmosphere is iconic and sculptural — Orthodox Easter reimagined as high porcelain ritual.

Three-quarter portrait of a woman seated at an old wooden table in a blooming garden, holding a hand close to her cheek and smiling dreamily into the camera. Her eyes sparkle with joy, and her relaxed posture leans slightly forward, capturing a moment of playful creativity.

She wears a flowing cotton dress in a floral pattern by Alena Akhmadullina, with puffed sleeves and embroidery along the neckline and cuffs. Her hair is loose, with soft waves falling across her shoulders. A silk ribbon is tied in her hair like a garland.

On the table beside her: a tray with small cupcakes, jars of watercolor, brushes, scattered eggs in pastel hues — some decorated with naïve folk patterns. A glass bottle holds flowering branches of apple blossom. A folded napkin embroidered with stars and birds adds a handmade touch.

The lighting is soft and natural, diffused through foliage, casting floral shadows on the fabric and her hands. Shot on a 50mm lens, cropped below the knees. The scene evokes intimacy, poetry, and joy — Easter as an act of art and tenderness.

Three-quarter portrait of a woman walking along a quiet side street near Patriarshiye Ponds in Moscow, just after Easter liturgy, holding a wicker basket in both hands close to her waist. She smiles openly, looking slightly toward the camera, her expression bright and natural — a moment of lightness and contentment. Her posture is relaxed, her shoulders slightly back, with an easy, graceful stride.

She wears a mid-length floral dress by Erdem — soft cream base with red and green botanical print, puffed sleeves to the elbow, and a cinched waist with flowing skirt. Over the dress, a linen trench coat in warm sand by Max Mara, open and belted loosely at the back. 

The woven basket in her hands is lined with a white embroidered napkin and filled with a tall panettone-style kulich — golden brown with thick white icing and rainbow sprinkles — surrounded by six hand-painted Easter eggs in traditional folk motifs. A thin beeswax candle rests diagonally inside, unlit but symbolic. The napkin is vintage linen with blue cross-stitch trim.

The background shows the red-brick wall and side entrance of a historic Orthodox church, slightly blurred. To her left, early cherry blossoms bloom above a wrought-iron fence, and the light pavement reflects soft morning sunshine. The lighting is fully natural — mid-morning, with sunlight from the right, casting gentle glow and subtle shadow on her face and coat.

Shot with a 50mm lens, cropped just below the knees, the composition centers on her joyful face and the basket, while the surroundings remain gently diffused. The overall mood is modern, real, and emotionally rich — a spontaneous moment of Orthodox Easter in the city, blending tradition and effortless style.

Three-quarter portrait of a woman standing in front of a mirrored table in a marble-lined conservatory, her hands gently holding a tall crystal vase filled with pastel-colored Easter eggs. She looks slightly above the lens, her smile warm but refined, her posture elegant and deliberate.

She wears a pale powder blue column dress by Del Core with subtle sequin embroidery, a sheer back panel, and a modest neckline. The sleeves fall straight to the wrist. Over her hair — a sleek cream silk scarf tied under the chin in a classic silhouette. Small crystal earrings glimmer in the light.

The table behind her features: one tall kulich on a glass pedestal, a pyramid-shaped paskha with the XB monogram in relief, six colored eggs on porcelain saucers, and an asymmetrical bouquet of cream tulips and dogwood. Crystal and silverware complete the scene.

Soft natural light from the glass ceiling reflects on the marble and her face. Shot at 50mm, cropped at the knees, the portrait centers on her and the vase. The mood is refined, fashion-forward, and contemplative — an Easter vignette of harmony, geometry and modern luxury.

Three-quarter portrait of a woman standing beside an Easter table inside an eclectic artist’s studio in central Moscow. The overall image blends humor, reverence, and curated visual folklore — a stylized but realistic interpretation of Pascha.

Easter Morning Stillness – A Private Celebration in Cashmere and Crystal

The Scene & Lighting:

A woman stands at a luxuriously arranged glass dining table in a modern, well-lit living room. The space is filled with soft, natural daylight—crisp and evenly diffused, characteristic of professional digital photography. There is no retro toning or artificial warmth; the colors remain balanced and true, enhancing the clean, neutral palette. Architectural moldings, framed artwork, and velvet curtains create a refined yet intimate atmosphere in the softly focused background. Shot with a 50mm lens and moderate depth of field, the composition keeps the woman and the tabletop in sharp focus while the setting gently recedes, allowing textures and forms to quietly unfold.

The Model & Styling:

She is dressed in an elegant yet cozy loungewear — a luxurious cream knit fine cashmere set consisting of a loose sweater and soft trousers. The silhouette is effortlessly relaxed and sophisticated, striking a perfect balance between modern luxury and lived-in comfort. Her presence feels natural and sincere, as if she has just approached the table or is about to pour tea. Her smile is tender and unposed, adding warmth to the carefully curated scene.

The Tabletop & Details:

The table is covered with a mirror-polished glass surface, reflecting the layered textures and delicate tones of a luxurious Orthodox Easter setting. At the center, sculptural Lalique crystal vases overflow with garden-style florals: pale pink ranunculus, lily of the valley, coral tulips, bright green viburnum, and tender branches of pussy willow. These blooms cascade and curve naturally, as if freshly gathered from a spring garden. Alongside them, small planters and brushed metal containers nest gold-dyed eggs on beds of moss, catching the light with subtle brilliance.

A porcelain figurine of a cherub emerging from an egg rests at the edge of the table, charming and quietly whimsical. The focal point is a delicate white porcelain plate holding a single Panettone-style Easter cake — cylindrical in shape, tall and elegant like the traditional Italian Christmas dessert — glazed with white icing that drips softly down the sides and finished with sugar flower details. Around it are painted green eggs with orange-red folk motifs, adding a vibrant, traditional accent.

An embroidered linen napkin lies neatly folded beside the plate, paired with vintage silverware. To one side stands a classic silver teapot and matching tea set, likely Georg Jensen, their surfaces gleaming in the daylight. A tall three-tiered dessert stand rises nearby, filled with jewel-toned macaroons, chocolate truffles, and gold-wrapped eggs, completing the quiet opulence of the spread.

Final Concept:

This editorial captures a serene moment of contemporary Easter celebration—private, elegant, and deeply rooted in ritual. The soft interplay of textures, florals, and heirloom details evoke intimacy and luxury without pretense. The woman’s refined casualwear, paired with high-end tableware and curated seasonal decor, tells a story of a modern home rooted in tradition, where beauty is quiet and Easter morning begins in stillness.

In the background, a clay jug with flowering cherry branches stands on the edge of the table, and a beeswax candle flickers in a low brass holder. The light is natural, diffused through a nearby window, and the moment is full of peace, tradition, and beauty.

Shot with a 50mm lens and shallow depth of field, the focus is on her hands and the eggs, while the background remains softly defined. This is a scene of quiet reverence — a symbolic gesture of care, spring, and the sacred joy of Pascha.

A woman stands at a wooden kitchen table, smiling warmly as she gently lowers a white egg into a glass jar filled with dark red onion peel dye. Around her are bowls and jars containing natural coloring agents: turmeric, beet juice, red cabbage, and spinach. Her expression is bright and concentrated, filled with joy and calm as she participates in the traditional Easter preparation.

She wears a soft cotton apron over a light embroidered blouse with wide sleeves, the cuffs rolled up just slightly to reveal her forearms stained faintly with dye. The apron is printed with a simple folk motif — wheat stalks and tiny tulips — in pale rust and gold tones, evoking a connection to the earth and ritual.

The table is covered with linen towels and drying racks. Several eggs are already dyed and arranged nearby: muted hues of mustard yellow, moss green, burgundy, dusty blue, and soft terracotta. Some eggs are wrapped in old lace or leaf prints for patterning. Beeswax candles flicker gently on the countertop, and in the background, a vase of blooming branches adds a soft vertical accent.

The kitchen is rustic and sun-filled, with morning light streaming through a nearby window and highlighting the steam rising from a pot of boiling dye on the stove. The atmosphere feels both serene and purposeful — a sacred domestic moment of creation rooted in ancient Orthodox tradition.

Shot on a 50mm lens with shallow depth of field, the woman’s hands and the egg-dyeing process are crisply in focus, while the background remains softly glowing. The scene evokes reverence, creativity, and the quiet beauty of ritual in preparation for Pascha.

One matching floral porcelain dessert plate features small tulip blossoms and three more painted eggs. A single clear vase holds a spring bouquet of tulips, daffodils, and lilac branches, arranged loosely with some petals gently falling to the table.

The cutlery is antique-style, made of silver with engraved handles, placed neatly to the side of each setting. The room is filled with a calm and luminous atmosphere, the early sun filtering through white linen curtains behind her, enhancing the sense of quiet joy and spiritual lightness.

Shot on a 50mm lens with medium depth of field — the woman and the table are in sharp focus, while the background softly fades, creating a painterly depth. The mood is warm, natural, and sacred — a celebration of Orthodox Easter as a personal, peaceful morning ritual in a sunlit home.

Woman in the Kitchen Dusting Kulich with Powdered Sugar

Concept Style: Neo-sacral Russian domestic ritual with warm realism

A gentle domestic moment unfolds in a sun-filled kitchen, where the sacred blends quietly with the everyday. A woman stands at a sturdy wooden table, leaning slightly forward, her left hand steadying a tall kulich while her right hand gently taps powdered sugar through a fine metal sieve. A soft cloud rises in the warm light, catching the sunbeams streaming in from the side window, turning the air into something almost luminous.

She wears a voluminous off-white linen blouse with wide sleeves and traditional floral embroidery in soft sky blue and pale red along the cuffs and shoulders — the blouse is untucked and relaxed, cinched at the waist with a narrow woven sash. Her skirt is full-length, made of natural raw cotton in warm cream. A light headscarf in ivory voile is loosely tied at the back of her head, framing her face softly. Her expression is peaceful and joyful, touched with a bright and radiant smile — the quiet joy of preparation.

The kulich in front of her is tall and cylindrical, resembling a traditional panettone — about 15 cm in diameter and 25 cm in height, with a golden-brown crust and visible raisins. Its surface is already covered in glossy white sugar glaze that drips down the sides in soft waves. As she dusts the top, a light layer of snowy powdered sugar settles over the glaze, adding contrast and texture.

The table is simply styled: a few more kulichi rest on ceramic plates, each with its own unique glaze pattern. Beside them lie hand-painted eggs in soft, dusty hues of rose, moss, and faded turquoise. A narrow glass vase with newly cut blooming branches — cherry or apple — stands nearby, the petals scattered lightly around it.

Shot with a 50mm lens, with natural digital lighting and soft focus on her hands and face. The atmosphere is calm, textured, and quietly sacred — not a performance, but a private act of love woven into tradition. The light, the flour, the wood, and the embroidery all speak in harmony: this is Easter preparation as inheritance and beauty.

three-quarter portrait of a woman seated upright on a low wooden bench covered with embroidered linen, in front of a deep red velvet curtain. Her torso is aligned with the camera, and she looks into the lens with a composed, ironic expression, now transformed into a joyful and radiant smile that lights up her face with warmth and ease. She wears a structured white cotton blouse with exaggerated puff sleeves and a high neckline fastened, tucked into a long pleated skirt in soft dove grey. On her head — a narrow red velvet headband with tiny pearl crosses sewn across the top. Her hands are placed neatly on her lap, holding a vintage porcelain plate with a vibrant assortment of 10–15 brightly painted Easter eggs in rich colors: scarlet, turquoise, golden yellow, emerald green, violet, and coral — each with expressive folk-inspired patterns and floral motifs. To her right stands a tall glass carafe filled with dark cherry juice, and beside her on the bench — a plate of traditional Orthodox kulich cakes glazed with sugar icing. The lighting is soft and frontal, giving her skin a porcelain glow and casting delicate shadows on the folds of fabric. Vertical framing, cropped at the knees.
